FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes cross-sectional data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study at Year 2 assessment (2018–2020) with N=9,008

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis reporting adjusted linear regression associations between problematic social media use and alcohol expectancies

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed research article published in BMC Public Health

FT_IC2.evidence: Problematic social media use (SMAQ measuring salience, mood modification, tolerance, withdrawal, conflict, relapse) is IV; paired with individual-trait-level outcome (alcohol expectancies: positive and negative beliefs about alcohol effects)

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports β coefficients in adjusted models: B=0.045 (95% CI 0.020–0.069) for positive expectancies; B=0.072 (95% CI 0.043–0.101) for negative expectancies

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric quantitative results reported for SM↔outcome relationship with regression coefficients and confidence intervals

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; EC7 applies only to longitudinal designs
notes: Time spent on social media alone was not associated with expectancies; the distinction between time and problematic use aligns with v6 FT-IC2 conceptual approach

FT_IC1.evidence: Prospective data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study at Baseline and Year 2 (2018–2020) with N=9,673 screen-using adolescents

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective longitudinal analysis using generalized logistic mixed effects models and generalized linear mixed effects models with ABCD data

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed research article published in BMC Public Health

FT_IC2.evidence: Problematic social media use (SMAQ) measured as outcome alongside problematic video game use and problematic mobile phone use (MPIQ); ACE score serves as IV paired with individual-trait-level construct (adverse childhood experiences—trauma exposure)

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports odds ratios for ACE associations: adjusted models show 4+ ACEs associated with 1.57 times higher odds of problematic mobile phone use (95% CI 1.22–2.03); supplemental analyses report continuous associations

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric quantitative results reported: OR=1.57 (95% CI 1.22–2.03) for mobile phone use; supplemental continuous models also reported

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design (baseline→Year 2); social media use (SMAQ) is outcome/DV in all analyses; no analysis anywhere in paper with SM as IV; meets all three EC7 criteria
notes: Problematic social media use was associated with ACEs in continuous supplemental models (Supplemental Table 3) but not in the binary logistic model (Table 2); authors attribute this to low prevalence (3.5%) in 11–12-year-olds

FT_IC1.evidence: Discusses and frames the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study as context for special issue

FT_IC5.evidence: This is a special issue introduction/editorial commentary. No original empirical analysis of ABCD data is presented. Text states: "The empirical reports in this special issue... showcase the work..." and describes what other papers in the special issue address, rather than conducting original analysis

notes: Exclusion code FT-EC3 (non-empirical / editorial commentary) applies at FT-IC5 stage
